title: Kitten Drooling While Teething: Normal Behavior or Cause for Concern?
meta_desc: Is your kitten drooling while teething? Learn why this happens, when it is normal, and how a photo can help your vet triage their dental health.
content_html:
Understanding Teething and Drooling

Between the ages of 3 and 6 months, kittens go through a significant developmental milestone: teething. During this time, their 26 deciduous (baby) teeth are replaced by 30 permanent adult teeth. As these new teeth push through the gums, they cause mild inflammation and irritation. This discomfort naturally stimulates the salivary glands, leading to the drooling you are observing. It is a completely normal biological response to the oral changes occurring in your kitten's mouth.

Is Teething Drool an Emergency?

The urgency level for drooling associated with teething is generally Low. While it can be messy and perhaps a bit startling for new pet owners, it is rarely a sign of a medical crisis. You should only be concerned if the drooling is paired with severe symptoms such as a complete refusal to eat, extreme lethargy, or a very foul odor emanating from the mouth. In most cases, your kitten will continue to play and eat normally despite the extra saliva.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

If you are unsure whether your kitten's gums look healthy, taking a clear photo can be an invaluable tool for your veterinary team. A close-up image of the gum line allows a professional to look for signs of 'retained deciduous teeth' (where the baby tooth won't fall out), excessive redness, or ulcers. Providing a visual aid helps your vet determine if your kitten requires a physical exam or if you can safely manage their teething symptoms with home monitoring and soft chew toys.

faqs.question: How long does kitten teething last?
faqs.answer: Kitten teething typically begins around 10 to 12 weeks of age and concludes by the time the kitten is 6 or 7 months old.

faqs.question: Is it normal for my kitten's breath to smell bad while teething?
faqs.answer: A mild 'metallic' or slightly unpleasant odor is common due to minor gum bleeding and saliva changes, but a very strong, putrid smell could indicate an infection.

faqs.question: Can I give my teething kitten human pain relief?
faqs.answer: No. Never give your kitten human medications like aspirin or ibuprofen, as these are highly toxic to cats. Stick to vet-approved chew toys or chilled wet food.
